questions.stem: A particle moves in one dimension under a potential $V(x)$. A point $x_0$ is an equilibrium position when
questions.explanation: The force is $F = -V'(x)$, so equilibrium, where the net force vanishes, requires $V'(x_0) = 0$: a stationary point of the potential. The value of $V$ at the point is irrelevant, since only its slope determines the force. Whether the equilibrium is stable or unstable is then settled separately by the second derivative $V''(x_0)$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: At an equilibrium point $x_0$ where $V'(x_0) = 0$, the equilibrium is stable when
questions.explanation: Stability requires that a small displacement produce a restoring force. Near $x_0$, $F = -V'(x) \approx -V''(x_0)(x - x_0)$, which points back toward $x_0$ only if $V''(x_0) > 0$, that is, if $x_0$ is a minimum of the potential. A maximum ($V''(x_0) < 0$) gives a force that pushes the particle away, hence instability. This is the familiar picture of a ball resting in a valley versus balanced on a hilltop.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Near a stable equilibrium at $x_0$, a particle of mass $m$ oscillates with angular frequency
questions.explanation: Expanding the potential about the minimum, $V(x) \approx V(x_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}V''(x_0)(x - x_0)^2$, the effective spring constant is $k = V''(x_0)$. The equation of motion $m\ddot{x} = -V''(x_0)(x - x_0)$ is simple harmonic with $\omega = \sqrt{V''(x_0)/m}$. The curvature of the potential plays the role of the spring stiffness; a sharper minimum (larger $V''$) gives a higher frequency.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle of mass $m = 2\ \text{kg}$ sits at a stable equilibrium where the potential has curvature $V''(x_0) = 8\ \text{N/m}$. What is its angular frequency of small oscillations?
questions.explanation: The small-oscillation frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{V''(x_0)/m} = \sqrt{8/2} = \sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{rad/s}$. The curvature acts as the spring constant $k = 8\ \text{N/m}$, and dividing by the mass before taking the square root is essential. Forgetting the square root would give $4$, the value of $\omega^2$, not $\omega$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A simple pendulum of length $L$ undergoes small oscillations under gravity $g$. Its angular frequency is
questions.explanation: The equation of motion $\ddot{\theta} = -\frac{g}{L}\sin\theta$ linearizes to $\ddot{\theta} = -\frac{g}{L}\theta$ for small angles, which is simple harmonic with $\omega^2 = g/L$. Hence $\omega = \sqrt{g/L}$, independent of the mass and of the amplitude (to leading order). A longer pendulum has a lower frequency. The inverted $\sqrt{L/g}$ is the period divided by $2\pi$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: For a system with $n$ degrees of freedom oscillating about a stable equilibrium, the number of normal modes is
questions.explanation: Linearizing about a stable equilibrium gives $n$ coupled linear equations, and diagonalizing them yields exactly $n$ independent normal modes, each a collective oscillation at its own eigenfrequency. The number of normal modes always equals the number of degrees of freedom. (Some of those modes can have zero frequency if the equilibrium is neutral in certain directions, but they still count.)
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A normal mode of a system of coupled oscillators is a motion in which
options: all parts oscillate at the same single frequency with fixed amplitude ratios
each part oscillates at a different frequency
only one part moves while the others stay fixed
questions.explanation: In a normal mode every coordinate oscillates sinusoidally at one common frequency, the modal eigenfrequency, with amplitudes in a fixed ratio and a fixed relative phase. The whole system moves as a single coherent pattern. A general motion is a superposition of normal modes, each contributing its own frequency, which is why a generic motion looks complicated even though each mode is simple.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two equal masses $m$ are connected in a line by three identical springs of stiffness $k$, with the outer springs fixed to walls (wall, spring, mass, spring, mass, spring, wall). The lower normal-mode frequency, the symmetric mode where both masses move together, is
questions.explanation: In the symmetric mode the two masses move identically, $x_1 = x_2$, so the central spring is never stretched and exerts no force. Each mass then feels only its wall spring, giving $m\ddot{x}_1 = -kx_1$ and frequency $\omega = \sqrt{k/m}$. This is the lower of the two frequencies, since the antisymmetric mode additionally stretches the central spring and so is stiffer.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: For the same two masses and three springs, the higher normal-mode frequency, the antisymmetric mode where the masses move in opposite directions ($x_1 = -x_2$), is
questions.explanation: With $x_1 = -x_2$, the central spring is stretched by $x_2 - x_1 = -2x_1$, so the force on mass 1 is $m\ddot{x}_1 = -kx_1 + k(x_2 - x_1) = -kx_1 - 2kx_1 = -3kx_1$. This gives $\omega = \sqrt{3k/m}$, the higher frequency. The opposed motion maximally stretches the coupling spring, stiffening the restoring force compared with the symmetric mode.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A bead of mass $m$ moves on a wire shaped so that its potential energy is $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ with $k = 18\ \text{N/m}$ and $m = 2\ \text{kg}$. The angular frequency of oscillation is
questions.explanation: For $V = \tfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ the second derivative is $V'' = k = 18\ \text{N/m}$, so $\omega = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{18/2} = \sqrt{9} = 3\ \text{rad/s}$. The potential is already quadratic, so no approximation is needed and the frequency is exact. The value $9$ is $\omega^2$, obtained by forgetting the square root.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The potential energy of a particle is $V(x) = x^3 - 3x$ (in suitable units). At which equilibrium point is the motion stable?
questions.explanation: Setting $V'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$ gives the equilibria $x = \pm 1$. The second derivative is $V''(x) = 6x$, so $V''(1) = 6 > 0$ (a minimum, stable) and $V''(-1) = -6 < 0$ (a maximum, unstable). The stable equilibrium is therefore $x = 1$. The point $x = 0$ is not an equilibrium at all, since $V'(0) = -3 \ne 0$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Normal coordinates are special combinations of the original coordinates that are useful because
options: they make the kinetic energy negative
they are always equal to the Cartesian coordinates
in terms of them the equations of motion decouple, each normal coordinate oscillating independently at its own eigenfrequency
they eliminate the potential energy entirely
questions.explanation: Normal coordinates are the linear combinations that simultaneously diagonalize the kinetic and potential energy quadratic forms. In these coordinates the coupled equations separate into independent simple-harmonic equations, one per mode, each of the form $\ddot{\eta}_i = -\omega_i^2\eta_i$. This turns a hard coupled problem into a set of trivial uncoupled oscillators, which is the whole point of the normal-mode method.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two equal masses $m$ are joined by a single spring of stiffness $k$ and are free to slide on a frictionless line, with no walls. One normal mode has zero frequency. What does that mode represent?
options: the spring vibrating while the masses stay fixed
uniform translation of both masses together (the centre of mass drifting), which has no restoring force
questions.explanation: With no walls, nothing resists the two masses sliding together at the same velocity: the centre of mass moves freely. That collective translation is a zero-frequency normal mode, since there is no restoring force, only constant drift. The other, nonzero mode is the relative vibration, in which the masses approach and recede and the spring does the restoring. Free systems generically have such zero modes, one for each unconstrained rigid displacement.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two equal masses $m$ are joined by a spring of stiffness $k$ and slide freely on a line. The nonzero normal mode is the relative vibration. Using the reduced mass $\mu = m/2$, its frequency is
questions.explanation: The relative coordinate $r = x_1 - x_2$ obeys $\mu\ddot{r} = -kr$ with reduced mass $\mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{m}{2}$. Hence $\omega = \sqrt{k/\mu} = \sqrt{k/(m/2)} = \sqrt{2k/m}$. The reduced mass being half the single mass raises the frequency by $\sqrt{2}$ relative to one mass on a fixed spring. Forgetting to use the reduced mass would give $\sqrt{k/m}$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two identical pendulums of length $L$ and mass $m$ are coupled by a weak spring of stiffness $k$. The in-phase normal mode (both swinging together) has frequency
questions.explanation: When both pendulums swing in phase, the distance between them never changes, so the coupling spring stays at its natural length and exerts no force. Each pendulum then behaves as an isolated simple pendulum, giving $\omega = \sqrt{g/L}$. The spring affects only the out-of-phase mode, where its stretching adds a stiffening term. This in-phase mode is therefore the lower of the two frequencies.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: For the same two coupled pendulums, the out-of-phase normal mode (swinging in opposite directions) has frequency
questions.explanation: In the out-of-phase mode the two bobs move oppositely, stretching the coupling spring by twice each displacement. The equation for one bob becomes $m\ddot{x}_1 = -\frac{mg}{L}x_1 - 2kx_1$, adding a term $2k/m$ to the squared frequency: $\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{L} + \frac{2k}{m}}$. This is the higher frequency, exceeding the in-phase $\sqrt{g/L}$. The gap between the two frequencies is what produces the slow beating of energy back and forth between the pendulums.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A simple pendulum has length $L = 2.5\ \text{m}$. Taking $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$, what is its angular frequency of small oscillations?
questions.explanation: Use $\omega = \sqrt{g/L} = \sqrt{10/2.5} = \sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{rad/s}$. The division gives $4$ inside the root, and the square root gives $2$. The corresponding period is $T = 2\pi/\omega = \pi\ \text{s} \approx 3.14\ \text{s}$. The value $4$ is $\omega^2$, the quantity before taking the square root.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: The potential energy of a particle is $V(x) = x^4 - 4x^2$ (in suitable units). How many stable equilibrium positions does it have?
questions.explanation: Setting $V'(x) = 4x^3 - 8x = 4x(x^2 - 2) = 0$ gives equilibria at $x = 0$ and $x = \pm\sqrt{2}$. The second derivative is $V''(x) = 12x^2 - 8$, so $V''(0) = -8 < 0$ (unstable) while $V''(\pm\sqrt{2}) = 24 - 8 = 16 > 0$ (stable). There are therefore two stable equilibria, the symmetric pair of wells at $x = \pm\sqrt{2}$, with an unstable maximum between them at the origin. This double-well shape is a standard model in physics.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Why is the harmonic (small-oscillation) approximation so widely useful for motion near a stable equilibrium?
options: Because any smooth potential looks quadratic near a minimum, so the leading restoring behaviour is always simple harmonic
Because all potentials are exactly quadratic
Because the equilibrium is always at the origin
questions.explanation: Taylor expanding any smooth potential about a minimum $x_0$, the constant term is irrelevant and the linear term vanishes (since $V'(x_0) = 0$), so the leading nontrivial term is the quadratic $\tfrac{1}{2}V''(x_0)(x - x_0)^2$. For small enough displacements this dominates, and the motion is simple harmonic with $\omega = \sqrt{V''(x_0)/m}$. The approximation works near any minimum, regardless of the full shape of the potential, which is why oscillators appear everywhere in physics.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: When two normal-mode frequencies of a coupled system are close but not equal, exciting one oscillator produces
options: immediate equipartition with no time dependence
no motion of the second oscillator ever
beats: the energy slowly transfers back and forth between the two oscillators
questions.explanation: A single-oscillator initial condition is a superposition of both normal modes with nearly equal frequencies $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$. The two modes drift in and out of phase at the slow beat frequency $\tfrac{1}{2}(\omega_2 - \omega_1)$, so the visible amplitude of each oscillator is modulated, and the energy sloshes periodically from one oscillator to the other. This beating is the hallmark of weakly coupled oscillators, seen for example in two pendulums sharing a soft spring.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A particle of mass $m = 2\ \text{kg}$ rests at a stable equilibrium where the potential has curvature $V''(x_0) = 8\ \text{N/m}$. Find the small-oscillation frequency.
questions.solution: The curvature plays the role of the spring constant, so $\omega = \sqrt{V''/m}$. The ratio $V''/m = 8/2 = $ {{1}}, and taking the square root gives $\omega = $ {{2}} radians per second.
questions.explanation: The ratio is $8/2 = 4$, and $\sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{rad/s}$. The square root must be taken at the end; $4$ is the value of $\omega^2$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Analyze the equilibria of the potential $V(x) = x^3 - 3x$ (in suitable units).
questions.solution: Setting $V'(x) = 3x^2 - 3 = 0$ gives the equilibria at $x = +1$ and $x = $ {{1}}. The second derivative is $V''(x) = 6x$, so the equilibrium at $x = 1$ is {{2}}, while the equilibrium at $x = -1$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The equilibria are $x = \pm 1$. Since $V''(1) = 6 > 0$ the point $x = 1$ is a minimum and stable, while $V''(-1) = -6 < 0$ makes $x = -1$ a maximum and unstable.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Two equal masses $m = 2\ \text{kg}$ are connected in a line by three identical springs of stiffness $k = 2\ \text{N/m}$, with the outer springs anchored to walls. Find the squared frequencies of the two normal modes.
questions.solution: In the symmetric mode the central spring is unstretched, giving $\omega_1^2 = k/m = $ {{1}}. In the antisymmetric mode the central spring is stretched, giving $\omega_2^2 = 3k/m = $ {{2}}. The {{3}} mode has the higher frequency.
questions.explanation: With $k = 2$ and $m = 2$: $\omega_1^2 = k/m = 1$ and $\omega_2^2 = 3k/m = 3$. The antisymmetric mode stretches the central spring and so is stiffer, giving the higher frequency.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: A simple pendulum has length $L = 2.5\ \text{m}$. Take $g = 10\ \text{m/s}^2$. Find its angular frequency and period of small oscillations.
questions.solution: The angular frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{g/L} = \sqrt{10/2.5} = \sqrt{4} = $ {{1}} radians per second. The period is $T = 2\pi/\omega$, which equals {{2}} (a multiple of $\pi$, write like 2\pi) seconds.
questions.explanation: Here $g/L = 10/2.5 = 4$, so $\omega = \sqrt{4} = 2\ \text{rad/s}$. The period is $T = 2\pi/2 = \pi\ \text{s} \approx 3.14\ \text{s}$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: State the stability test for a one-dimensional equilibrium and the resulting oscillation frequency.
questions.solution: An equilibrium occurs where $V'(x_0) = 0$. It is stable when $V''(x_0)$ is {{1}}, corresponding to a minimum of the potential. Near such a minimum the particle of mass $m$ oscillates with angular frequency $\omega = $ {{2}}.
questions.explanation: Stability needs $V''(x_0) > 0$ (a minimum), so the restoring force points back toward equilibrium. The effective spring constant is $V''(x_0)$, giving $\omega = \sqrt{V''(x_0)/m}$.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics

questions.stem: Describe the two normal modes of two identical pendulums (length $L$, mass $m$) coupled by a spring of stiffness $k$.
questions.solution: In the in-phase mode the bobs swing together, the spring stays slack, and the frequency is $\omega = \sqrt{g/L}$, the {{1}} of the two. In the out-of-phase mode the spring stretches and the frequency rises to $\omega = $ {{2}}. The slow exchange of energy between the pendulums when one is set going is called {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The in-phase mode leaves the spring slack, giving the lower frequency $\sqrt{g/L}$. The out-of-phase mode stretches the spring, adding $2k/m$ under the root to give $\sqrt{g/L + 2k/m}$. The two close frequencies interfere to produce beats, the periodic transfer of energy between the pendulums.
questions.source: in the style of Goldstein, Classical Mechanics
